questions.question: Explain the difference between Functional and Non-Functional requirements with examples.
keyPoints: Functional requirements describe what the system must do (specific features/functions)
Non-functional requirements describe how the system should perform (quality attributes)
Example functional: System should allow users to login and logout
Example non-functional: System should respond within 2 seconds

questions.question: Describe any four techniques used for Requirements Elicitation.
keyPoints: Interviews - one-on-one discussions with stakeholders
Brainstorming - group creativity sessions to generate ideas
Prototyping - creating early models to visualize requirements
Observation - watching users perform their tasks
Questionnaires/Surveys - written questions distributed to stakeholders
Joint Application Development (JAD) - collaborative workshops

questions.question: What is Requirements Validation? Explain its importance.
keyPoints: Requirements validation ensures requirements accurately represent stakeholder needs
It checks for completeness, consistency, and testability
Detects errors early before costly development phases
Validates that requirements are unambiguous and feasible
Involves techniques like reviews, prototyping, and test case development

questions.question: Write a User Story for an Online Banking System.
keyPoints: User Story format: As a [user], I want [goal], so that [benefit]
Example: As a bank customer, I want to view my account balance online, so that I can check my finances anytime
Should include acceptance criteria defining when the story is complete
Should be concise and focused on user value

questions.question: Explain the concept of Requirements Traceability and its benefits.
keyPoints: Traceability links requirements to design, code, and test cases throughout development
Forward traceability: requirements to implementation
Backward traceability: implementation back to source requirements
Benefits: ensures all requirements are implemented, easier impact analysis, helps in testing
Essential for maintenance and regulatory compliance

questions.question: What are the key characteristics of a good SRS document?
keyPoints: Complete - includes all requirements
Consistent - no contradictions between requirements
Unambiguous - clear, single interpretation
Testable - requirements can be verified
Modifiable - easy to make changes
Traceable - can be traced to source

questions.question: Explain the Requirements Engineering process and its phases.
keyPoints: Phase 1: Feasibility Study - assess if project is viable
Phase 2: Requirements Elicitation and Analysis - gather requirements from stakeholders
Phase 3: Requirements Specification - document requirements in SRS
Phase 4: Requirements Validation - verify requirements are correct
Phase 5: Requirements Management - manage changes throughout project

questions.question: Explain in detail the Requirements Elicitation process and discuss various techniques used for gathering requirements.
keyPoints: Requirements Elicitation is the process of discovering requirements from stakeholders
Techniques include: Interviews (structured/unstructured), Brainstorming sessions, Joint Application Development (JAD), Prototyping, Observation, Questionnaire surveys, Document analysis
Each technique has advantages and limitations
Interview: good for detailed info but time-consuming
Brainstorming: generates many ideas but may lack focus
Prototyping: helps visualize but may create false expectations
JAD: collaborative but requires key stakeholders available
Success depends on proper stakeholder identification and communication

questions.question: What is an SRS document? Explain the recommended structure of SRS as per IEEE 830 standard.
keyPoints: SRS (Software Requirements Specification) is the official document describing all system requirements
IEEE 830 provides guidelines for SRS preparation
Structure includes: 1) Introduction (purpose, scope, definitions)
2) Overall Description (user characteristics, product perspective, constraints)
3) Specific Requirements (functional requirements, interface requirements, performance requirements, design attributes)
Functional requirements should be organized by features not by data
Non-functional requirements should be quantifiable
Appendices may include glossary, acronyms, references

questions.question: Discuss the different types of Non-Functional requirements with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Non-functional requirements specify quality attributes rather than specific features
Types: Performance (response time, throughput), Security (authentication, encryption)
Reliability (failure rate, availability), Usability (user-friendliness, learning time)
Scalability (ability to handle growth), Maintainability (ease of modification)
Efficiency (resource utilization), Portability (platform compatibility)
Each type can be quantified with metrics for testing
Example: Performance - system should process 100 transactions per second
Example: Security - data should be encrypted using AES-256

questions.question: Explain Requirements Management. Discuss the importance of managing changes to requirements and techniques for prioritization.
keyPoints: Requirements Management involves controlling changes to requirements throughout the project
Changes are inevitable due to evolving business needs, market conditions, or stakeholder feedback
Without proper management, uncontrolled changes lead to scope creep and project failure
Change Control Process: Submit change request → Assess impact → Approve/Reject → Implement → Verify
Prioritization techniques: MoSCoW (Must, Should, Could, Won't), Kano model, Wiegers method
Traceability matrix helps track requirements across lifecycle
Version control tracks changes to requirements documents
